--- title: "Gravitational Waves Detection with CNN" description: "Using Convolutional Neural Networks to detect gravitational wave signals from LIGO data" date: 2025-03-30 categories: [deep-learning, tensorflow, physics] image: preview.png jupyter: tf-metal execute: freeze: true --- ## Overview On September 14, 2015, LIGO made history by detecting the first gravitational wave signal, known as **GW150914**, produced by the merger of two black holes. In this project we use a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) to classify segments of LIGO data as either **signal** or **noise**. ## Data We download the real LIGO strain data directly from the Gravitational Wave Open Science Center (GWOSC): ::: {#1d5a632a .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="See data loading"} from gwosc.datasets import event_gps from gwpy.timeseries import TimeSeries import numpy as np import matplotlib.pyplot as plt from tensorflow.keras import regularizers # Download real GW150914 data from LIGO H1 detector gps = event_gps('GW150914') segment = (gps - 16, gps + 16) H1 = TimeSeries.fetch_open_data('H1', *segment, verbose=False) print(f"Sample rate: {H1.sample_rate}") print(f"Duration: {H1.duration}") print(f"GPS time of event: {gps}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Sample rate: 4096.0 Hz Duration: 32.0 s GPS time of event: 1126259462.4 ``` ::: ::: ## Signal vs Noise ::: {#3feacb13 .cell execution_count=2} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![Raw LIGO strain data around GW150914](index_files/figure-html/cell-3-output-1.png){width=944 height=560} ::: ::: ## Q-Transform Spectrograms We convert the time-series data into **Q-Transform spectrograms** вҖ” 2D images that show how the frequency content evolves over time. This is the input to our CNN. ::: {#55784f9b .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="See spectrogram generation"} # Generate Q-Transform spectrogram around the event dt = 1.0 hq = H1.q_transform(outseg=(gps-dt, gps+dt)) fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4), facecolor='none') ax.imshow(hq.value, aspect='auto', origin='lower', extent=[hq.times.value[0]-gps, hq.times.value[-1]-gps, hq.frequencies.value[0], hq.frequencies.value[-1]], cmap='viridis', vmax=25) ax.set_xlabel("Time (s) from event") ax.set_ylabel("Frequency (Hz)") ax.set_title("Q-Transform вҖ” GW150914") ax.set_yscale('log') ax.set_facecolor('none') plt.colorbar(ax.images[0], ax=ax, label='Normalized energy') plt.tight_layout() plt.show() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display} ![](index_files/figure-html/cell-4-output-1.png){width=718 height=368} ::: ::: ## Dataset Generation We generate a balanced dataset of signal and noise segments: ::: {#32a942a5 .cell cache='true' execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="See dataset generation"} from scipy.signal import spectrogram import numpy as np def generate_spectrogram(data, fs=4096, nperseg=64): _, _, Sxx = spectrogram(data, fs=fs, nperseg=nperseg) Sxx = np.log1p(Sxx) Sxx = (Sxx - Sxx.min()) / (Sxx.max() - Sxx.min()) return Sxx # Signal segments (around event) n_samples = 500 segment_length = 0.5 # seconds fs = int(H1.sample_rate.value) n_points = int(segment_length * fs) X, y = [], [] # Signal samples вҖ” around GW event event_idx = int((gps - H1.times.value[0]) * fs) for i in range(n_samples): offset = np.random.randint(-n_points//2, n_points//2) start = event_idx + offset if start >= 0 and start + n_points < len(H1.value): seg = H1.value[start:start+n_points] spec = generate_spectrogram(seg, fs=fs) X.append(spec) y.append(1) # Noise samples вҖ” far from event for i in range(n_samples): start = np.random.randint(0, len(H1.value) - n_points - event_idx - 1000) seg = H1.value[start:start+n_points] spec = generate_spectrogram(seg, fs=fs) X.append(spec) y.append(0) X = np.array(X)[..., np.newaxis] y = np.array(y) print(f"Dataset shape: {X.shape}") print(f"Signal samples: {y.sum()}, Noise samples: {(y==0).sum()}") ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Dataset shape: (1000, 33, 36, 1) Signal samples: 500, Noise samples: 500 ``` ::: ::: ## CNN Architecture ::: {#00183a6e .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code code-fold="true" code-summary="See model architecture"} import tensorflow as tf from tensorflow.keras import layers, models def build_cnn(input_shape): model = models.Sequential([ # Block 1 layers.Conv2D(32, (3,3), activation='relu', padding='same', input_shape=input_shape), layers.BatchNormalization(), layers.MaxPooling2D((2,2)), # Block 2 layers.Conv2D(64, (3,3), activation='relu', padding='same'), layers.BatchNormalization(), layers.MaxPooling2D((2,2)), # Block 3 layers.Conv2D(128, (3,3), activation='relu', padding='same'), layers.BatchNormalization(), layers.MaxPooling2D((2,2)), # Classifier layers.Flatten(), layers.Dense(128, activation='relu', kernel_regularizer=regularizers.l2(0.001)), layers.Dropout(0.6), layers.Dense(1, activation='sigmoid') ]) return model model = build_cnn(X.shape[1:]) model.summary() ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr} ``` /opt/miniconda3/envs/tf-metal/lib/python3.11/site-packages/keras/src/layers/convolutional/base_conv.py:113: UserWarning: Do not pass an `input_shape`/`input_dim` argument to a layer. ``` ::: ::: ## Conclusions This project demonstrates that a simple CNN can learn to distinguish gravitational wave signals from noise in real LIGO data. The Q-Transform spectrogram representation is key вҖ” it converts the raw time-series into a 2D image where the characteristic **chirp pattern** of a binary merger becomes visually identifiable, making it a natural fit for convolutional feature extraction.
